license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Certificate chain built
entries.summary: A certificate chain was constructed for validation.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Cryptographic API (CAPI2) constructs a certificate chain for validation. Key fields: SubjectCert (the leaf certificate being validated), IssuerCert, ChainContext, and Result (success or failure with error code). This event fires for every certificate validation the operating system performs — TLS connections, code signing verification, document signing, etc. At baseline volume it is extremely noisy. Its forensic value lies in failures: a chain build failure indicates a certificate that cannot be verified against a trusted root, which may indicate use of a self-signed cert, a private CA not installed in the trust store, or a revoked/expired certificate. Note: this channel is disabled by default and must be enabled via wevtutil or Event Viewer before events are collected.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

investigationPivots: Filter for Result=failure — successful chain builds are expected and high volume
SubjectCert issuer from an unknown or private CA may indicate an attacker-controlled certificate authority
Chain build failures for code signing certificates on newly executed binaries are high priority
commonFalsePositives: Any TLS connection or code signing validation generates this event — baseline volume is very high
Self-signed certificates used by internal tools or development environments

entries.title: Certificate chain policy verified
entries.summary: A certificate chain was evaluated against a chain policy (e.g., SSL, authenticode).
entries.details: Generated when a constructed certificate chain is evaluated against a specific chain policy — for example, the SSL policy (for TLS), the authenticode policy (for code signing), or the base policy. Key fields: ChainContext, PolicyOID (the policy being checked), Result, and the specific policy error flags. This event reveals why a certificate was accepted or rejected at the policy level, beyond just chain construction. From a security standpoint, a chain policy verification failure on an authenticode policy for a recently executed binary indicates unsigned or improperly signed code. In TLS inspection and proxy detection scenarios, a chain policy failure may indicate a root CA mismatch caused by SSL interception. Note: requires CAPI2 operational log to be enabled.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

investigationPivots: PolicyOID identifies the context — Authenticode policy failures (1.3.6.1.4.1.311.2.1.29) on recently executed binaries are highest priority
Result error codes (CERT_E_REVOKED, CERT_E_EXPIRED, CERT_E_UNTRUSTEDROOT) indicate specific failure types
CERT_E_UNTRUSTEDROOT on a TLS connection may indicate SSL interception by a proxy not in the machine trust store
commonFalsePositives: Internal TLS services using private CA certificates not in the Windows root store
Expired certificates on older internal systems that have not been renewed

entries.title: CRL or OCSP object retrieved
entries.summary: The crypto subsystem fetched a Certificate Revocation List (CRL) or OCSP response from a URL.
entries.details: Generated when CryptRetrieveObjectByUrl is called to fetch a CRL, OCSP staple, or certificate from a remote URL as part of revocation checking. Key fields: URL (the CRL Distribution Point or OCSP responder URL being contacted) and Result. This event is useful for detecting adversarial certificate infrastructure: if a binary's signing certificate references a CRL Distribution Point (CDP) at an attacker-controlled domain, this event will record the URL fetch when the binary is executed or verified. An unusual CDP or OCSP URL (non-Microsoft, non-major-CA) correlated with execution of a newly dropped binary may indicate use of an attacker-signed certificate. Also useful for detecting environments where certificate revocation checking is failing (Result=failure) because CRL endpoints are unreachable.

investigationPivots: URL field is the primary pivot — CRL Distribution Points at unknown or attacker-controlled domains indicate a malicious signing certificate
Correlate the URL's domain with threat intel to identify known attacker certificate infrastructure
Result=failure for all CRL fetches indicates the endpoint cannot perform revocation checking — a gap that attackers may exploit
commonFalsePositives: Routine CRL and OCSP fetches for all TLS connections and code signing verifications — very high volume
CRL fetch failures when endpoints are offline or when firewall rules block access to CRL Distribution Points

entries.title: Certificate revocation check performed
entries.summary: A revocation check was performed against a certificate in a chain.
entries.details: Generated when Windows performs a revocation check (CRL or OCSP) against a specific certificate. Key fields: CertificateThumbprint, CertificateIssuer, RevocationCheckResult, and the CRL/OCSP endpoint used. A result of CERT_E_REVOKED indicates the certificate is on a revocation list — this is a critical finding when correlated with recently executed code, as it means a revoked signing certificate was used to sign a binary. Adversaries occasionally use certificates that were valid at signing time but have since been revoked, or certificates revoked due to compromise. CERT_E_REVOKED combined with an execution event for the corresponding binary is a strong indicator of malicious signed code. Note: requires CAPI2 operational log to be enabled.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

investigationPivots: CERT_E_REVOKED on a code signing certificate is critical — identify what binary triggered the check and retrieve its hash
CertificateThumbprint links this event to a specific certificate — search for the thumbprint across threat intel databases
Correlate with EID 30 to identify the CRL endpoint that confirmed revocation
commonFalsePositives: Revocation checks against expired or decommissioned internal CA certificates
Applications that embed old signing certificates for legacy compatibility and trigger revocation events on startup

entries.title: X.509 chain policy verified
entries.summary: An X.509 certificate chain was evaluated against a trust or application policy.
entries.details: Generated when an X.509 certificate chain is evaluated against a configured trust or application policy, such as the Basic Constraints policy, Enhanced Key Usage (EKU) policy, or a custom application policy. Key fields: CertificateThumbprint, PolicyOID, and Result. Failures indicate a certificate is being used outside its intended scope — for example, a certificate with an EKU of 'Client Authentication' being used for 'Code Signing', which is a common indicator of certificate misuse. In attacks involving stolen or forged certificates, EKU mismatches may be present if the attacker used a certificate not intended for the context in which they're deploying it. Note: requires CAPI2 operational log to be enabled.

prerequisites.description: The CAPI2 operational log channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events are collected.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Install Root Certificate

investigationPivots: PolicyOID failures indicating EKU mismatch suggest a certificate being used outside its intended purpose
Combine CertificateThumbprint with EID 11 and EID 13 events for the same certificate to build the full validation picture
A new trusted root CA certificate being installed (visible via EID 70 for basic constraints) warrants immediate investigation
commonFalsePositives: Internal PKI certificates with custom EKUs not matching expected Windows policy checks
Test certificates in development environments with non-standard policy settings
